title: A Fully Integrated D-Band I/Q Homodyne Transceiver Enabling a Multi-Gigabit Wired Link
authors: Vasileios Manouras, Yannis Papananos, Franz Dielacher
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents a fully integrated direct-conversion I/Q transmitter and receiver fabricated in a 130-nm SiGe BiCMOS process and experimentally validated through a short-range on-board link. At a carrier frequency of 120 GHz, the prototype has demonstrated data transmission up to 50 Gbps over a 50-mm microstrip trace on a standard multilayer printed circuit board. This is accomplished using 16-QAM modulation with a measured EVM of 14.7%. Although the PCB interconnect was intentionally implemented as a low-cost, non-optimized evaluation platform, the achieved link energy efficiency of 19.2 pJ/bit underscores the robustness of the proposed D-band transceiver and validates its suitability for high-speed intra-package and module-level communication at sub-THz frequencies.

title: Adversarial Robustness in Multilingual and Code-Mixed NLP: Insights from Indian Language Settings
authors: Arunkumar G, Deepa M
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Despite the widespread use of NLP technologies have become widely used in multilingual and code-mixed settings, adversarial robustness efforts have predominantly concentrated on monolingual English, resulting in the inadequacy of the existing literature to identify adversarially vulnerable aspects within linguistically varied systems, especially Indian languages. This paper fills this gap via an extensive review of adversarial robustness in multilingual and code-mixed NLP systems with a particular emphasis on Indian language applications as high-variation deployment contexts. By systematically selecting studies, 108 papers spanning from 2019-2025 were selected among over 400 candidates across different NLP and machine learning conferences. The survey consolidates findings on six aspects: (i) linguistic characteristics of multilingual and code-mixed texts; (ii) taxonomy of adversarial attacks considering text granularity and linguistic nature; (iii) defense approaches including preprocessing, adversarial training, representation level, prompt-level, and detection-based schemes; (iv) evaluation criteria and benchmark datasets; (v) cross-lingual transfer dynamics under adversarial and distribution shifts; and (vi) ethical concerns. The study reveals three major insights. First, fragility of tokenizers and code-switching make a compound vulnerability that is currently not addressed by defenses. Secondly, the adversarial weaknesses of language models can be transferred across languages via cross-lingual transfer, thus affecting Indian low-resource languages more than other languages. Third, the present evaluation approaches that use monolingual metrics specific to each task are likely to under-estimate the problem of robustness in multilingual settings wherein adversarial-specific metrics provide more insights. Moreover, there are no benchmark tests developed specifically for assessing robustness for code-mixed language pairs in India. In terms of numbers, previous literature highlights attack success rates ranging from 36.8% to 41.1% in Indian language models against word-level attacks, adversarial confirmation bias failure rates of 14%, and 5-15 point safety alignment gaps between instruction-tuned models in English and Hindi. Thus, addressing these problems is critical for the robust development of NLP applications. Contributions include a taxonomy for attack approaches in two dimensions with Indian language examples, cross-mapping of attack approaches to defensive techniques, and vulnerability analysis of adversarial attacks against ten Indian NLP resources.

title: Boosting the Performance of Decentralized Federated Learning via Catalyst Acceleration_supp1-3688672.pdf
authors: Qinglun Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning has emerged as an alternative to centralized architectures due to its faster training, privacy preservation, and reduced communication overhead. In decentralized communication, the server aggregation phase in Centralized Federated Learning shifts to the client side, which means that clients connect with each other in a peer-to-peer manner. However, compared to the centralized mode, data heterogeneity in Decentralized Federated Learning will cause larger variances between aggregated models, which leads to slow convergence in training and poor generalization performance in tests. To address these issues, we introduce Catalyst Acceleration and propose an acceleration Decentralized Federated Learning algorithm called DFedCata. It consists of two main components: the Moreau envelope function, which primarily addresses parameter inconsistencies among clients caused by data heterogeneity, and Nesterov's extrapolation step, which accelerates the aggregation phase. Theoretically, we prove the optimization error bound and generalization error bound of the algorithm, providing a further understanding of the nature of the algorithm and the theoretical perspectives on the hyperparameter choice. Empirically, we demonstrate the advantages of the proposed algorithm in both convergence speed, computational cost, and generalization performance on CIFAR10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet with various non-iid data distributions. Moreover, extensive experiments are conducted to validate the theoretical properties of DFedCata, showing strong consistency between theory and empirical observations. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/zzylyxx/DFedCata</uri>.

title: Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Network-Based Traffic Sensing Approach for Satellite Communication Networks
authors: Shuotong Wei, Tao Dong, Zhihui Liu, Hang Di, Qiwei Zhang, Haijun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Satellite communication networks, characterized by the advantages of seamless global coverage and ubiquitous access, have emerged as a main focus of current research. However, satellite network topology changes dynamically and burst traffic is spatio-temporal coupling, causing the satellite network traffic sensing problem with high overhead and low accuracy. Therefore, this paper proposes a spatio-temporal graph neural network-based satellite network traffic sensing approach (STSA) to collect data efficiently and predict traffic precisely. The study designs in-band network telemetry (INT)-based Hamilton cycle to reduce the number of repeated visits to satellite nodes, reducing the redundancy of data collection, and construct spatio-temporal graph of satellite network topology and traffic. STSA takes full advantage of the spatio-temporal dependence of topology and traffic to realize satellite network traffic sensing. The satellite network state view is generated by mapping the spatio-temporal characteristics of historical traffic to the next period. Simulation shows that STSA effectively reduces sensing overhead and improves accuracy compared to the traditional traffic sensing algorithm based on the Euler cycle and long short-term memory (LSTM). The proposed STSA provides traffic data for satellite network routing, enhancing the efficiency of satellite network management and control.

title: Resource-Efficient Plastic Classification System Using Low-Cost NIR Spectroscopy and Edge AI: A Case Study on Sustainable Toy Recycling
authors: Tzu-Ling Kuo, Hsiao-Wei Hu, Hung-Kai Huang, Tzu-Yang Lin, Chong-Wei Wang, Chun-Wei Yu, Bo-Wei Chen
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Accurate identification of heterogeneous toy plastics is a key bottleneck in decentralized recycling, yet conventional spectroscopic sorting often relies on expensive instrumentation or centralized computation. Addressing the challenge of AI-augmented sensor interfaces, this paper presents a resource-efficient, edge-AI plastic classification system that demonstrates how lightweight machine learning can compensate for the inherent limitations of consumer-grade spectral hardware by coupling a low-cost multi-spectral NIR sensing interface with an embedded-deployable learning model through a system-level co-design approach. The sensing front-end utilizes an AS7265x-basedmultispectral module providing 18 discrete bands from 410 nm to 940 nm. To ensure measurement consistency and embody circular economy principles, the sensor is integrated into a modular, light-shielding enclosure constructed from repurposed ABS-based blocks (LEGO elements), which maintains a fixed measurement geometry and stabilizes the optical environment. To compensate for the limited spectral resolution of cost-effective hardware, a radial basis function (RBF) kernel Support Vector Machine (SVM) is optimized for constrained deployment. The system was evaluated on a dataset of 6,650 Raman-verified, independent physical fragments (PP, PE, ABS, and “Others”). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed SVM achieves a theoretical laboratory accuracy of 99.17% (0.9933 macro F1-score), while maintaining a practical field-test benchmark of 75.00% when deployed on uncleaned, aged post-consumer plastics. On an Arduino Uno R4 Minima platform, the system delivers a measured model inference latency of 118.6 ms. Combined with the 140 ms sensor integration time, the total end-to-end latency is approximately 258.6 ms. Based on analytical estimation from hardware datasheets, the energy consumption per classification cycle is approximately 0.0513 J, enabling real-time, offline sorting without cloud connectivity. This work demonstrates how AI-fostered, resource-efficient sensor–AI co-design can extend low-cost multispectral sensing toward practical, decentralized waste management.

title: Time-based 8TD SRAM CIM Macro with Low PVT Sensitivity for Edge Devices
authors: Andrea Fasolino, Pio Esposito, Rosalba Liguori, Luigi Di Benedetto, Alfredo Rubino, Gian Domenico Licciardo
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
abstract: Computing-in-Memory (CIM) promises to overcome the von Neumann bottleneck; however, analog CIM designs face challenges such as read disturbance, nonlinearity, and process-voltage-temperature (PVT) variations. Therefore, this paper presents an 8-transistor SRAM CIM macro with built-in PVT robustness to tackle these challenges. The proposed dynamic cascode SRAM bitcell utilizes stacked high- and low-Vth NMOS access devices and multilevel current-mode word-line driving to enhance read stability and achieve up to 54-fold lower bitcell current variability without requiring separate read/write ports. A subthreshold current-starved ring oscillator and a transmission-gate counter perform time-based analog-to-digital conversion of MAC results, achieving an integral nonlinearity of <0.5 LSB across PVT corners without calibration circuits. Post-layout results in TSMC 65 nm CMOS LP technology show a storage density of 241 kb/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and an area efficiency of 31.32 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> for 2-bit inputs and 1-bit weights. The 64×128 macro maintains functionality from 0.65 V to 1.2 V and yields <0.8% MNIST accuracy loss, demonstrating robust, energy-efficient CIM for edge AI.

title: Global Sensitivity Analysis of Whole-Body Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Model Parameters in [Lu]Lu-PSMA-I&#x0026;T Radioligand Therapy: Key Determinants of Individual Kidney and Tumor Absorbed Dose Uncertainty and Implications for Digital Twin Development_supp1-3690503.pdf
authors: Deni Hardiansyah
publish: N/A
abstract: Understanding how uncertainties in physiological parameters propagate into the uncertainty of estimated absorbed doses (eADs) is desirable for optimizing individualized radiopharmaceutical therapy (RPT). In this study, we applied global sensitivity analysis (GSA) to a published whole-body physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model, developed using biokinetic data from 13 patients treated with [<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">177</sup>Lu]Lu-PSMA-I&T, to identify influential parameters on the individual eAD uncertainty. Model inputs included patient-specific parameters such as receptor densities (R₀), blood flow densities (f), and release rates (λ) across relevant organs, with their uncertainties and correlations incorporated. A GSA method extended for dependent parameters (GSADPK) was employed to compute main effects (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub>) and total effects (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Ti</sub>), with kidney and tumor eADs as the outputs of interest. Overall, kidney eADs showed lower uncertainty than tumors. For kidneys, receptor density emerged as the dominant contributor (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub>, median [range] = 0.40 [0.10–0.58]), followed by blood flow density (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub> = 0.27 [0.12–0.48]). In tumors, receptor density was also found as the primary determinant (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub> = 0.42 [0.02–0.80]), with tumor release rate as a secondary contributor (S<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sub> = 0.04 [0.00–0.67]). These findings highlight that accurate estimation of receptor density and blood flow density could substantially reduce eAD uncertainty. By identifying the physiological parameters most critical for dose prediction, this work provides a foundation for digital twin–based frameworks.
