title: Smart Cradle: An IoT-Based System for Intelligent Infant Monitoring
authors: Adithiyaa D, Lakshhmi Narayanan T, Gokulakkrishna Saravanan, Parvathy A K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of an IoT-based smart monitoring system that is fitted onto a baby's cradle and performs predefined, automated actions to help keep the baby safe and to help soothe the baby without the physical intervention of caregivers. An ESP32 microcontroller is used as the main controller to track key environmental conditions. The BME680 sensor monitors air quality and temperature and soil moisture sensor is used to detect diaper wetness. Baby crying is identified using a sound sensor. The system is triggered automatically whenever any sensors crosses its set threshold limit and as a response it performs one of the following actions, which includes switching on the fan, swinging the cradle using a servo motor, sending alerts to the caregiver, and playing lullabies to calm the baby. Also, to help caregivers monitor the baby remotely at any time, an ESP32 CAM module is used to stream live video of the cradle. The system is designed so that it can be operated through manual remote control in the Blynk platform, and even a web dashboard is provided for real-time data monitoring. Overall, this work presents a complete and practical childcare solution by combining embedded hardware with cloud-based IoT technology.

title: Real-Time GR-Based Shoulder Dislocation Risk and Strength Monitoring System Using Arduino for Rehabilitation and Preventive Care
authors: Neeththi Aadithiya B, Nivasan K, Praveen Kumar K, Nithishkumar M
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS)
abstract: One of the most recurrent musculoskeletal injuries is shoulder dislocation, which, if not identified quickly, can result in pain, instability, and long-term issues. Expensive imaging procedures like MRIs and X-rays are a major part of current diagnostic procedures, but they are not always practicable for ongoing monitoring or preventive care. In the presented research a non-invasive, portable shoulder dislocation monitoring device that tracks abnormal muscle activity around the shoulder joint using surface Electromyography (sEMG) sensors is thus proposed. The presented system identifies the dislocation, weariness, and strain by using RMS-based threshold detection. The analysis of the real-time recorded signals is processed via a Raspberry Pi, which is actually amplified and digitalized using an ADC. The proposed method can be readily utilized by athletes and can be utilized in physiotherapy clinics and rehabilitation centers as the system is user friendly. Overall the Proposed system will be an alternate solution for the expensive scans and will promote the earlier detection.

title: Distribution Network Disaster Prediction Based on Adaptive Random Forest Algorithm under Extreme Weather
authors: Jinfeng Ye, Bin Wu, Xuan Lin, Chaoyang Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: To address the insufficient accuracy of distribution network disaster prediction under frequent extreme weather events, this paper proposes a prediction method based on Adaptive Random Forest (ARF). First, historical distribution network operation data under extreme weather events are cleaned and standardized, and key input variables are selected through correlation analysis. Then, an ARF model with dynamic adjustment capabilities is constructed. Its core lies in online optimization of the number and depth of decision trees based on changes in the distribution of input data, thereby improving the model's adaptability to non-stationary and high-noise environments. The model training employs a time-series cross-validation strategy to balance generalization ability and stability. Experiments on distribution network data show that the proposed method achieves an accuracy of 92.3 %, a recall of 89.7 %, and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 90.8 %, significantly outperforming mainstream models such as XGBoost and LightGBM. Furthermore, it demonstrates excellent temporal stability and robustness under extreme events of varying intensities, including typhoons and rainstorms. This research confirms that the adaptive mechanism of ARF can accurately capture dynamic fault modes, providing reliable technical support for improving the resilience and emergency response capabilities of distribution networks.

title: Simulation-Based Dynamic Spectrum Management Using Deep Q-Learning for Cognitive Radios
authors: Aryan Routray, Aishik Tokdar, Abhijay Dhar, Nirmala Paramanandham
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: The ever-increasing demands of wireless communications and the rapid proliferation of wireless devices have made the efficient and dependable use of the radio spectrum more important than ever. Unlike traditional Cognitive Radio (CR) systems, which often do not have adaptive decision-making for dynamic, real-time management of the spectrum under conditions of unpredictable interference and uncertain channel occupancy, this paper addresses the problem with an autonomous and self-adjusting Cognitive Radio framework which adapts spectrum access based on Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) through simulated environments. For this purpose, the designed system incorporates a Deep Q-Network (DQN) model, which derives optimal policies associated with channel access based on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), the channel occupancy history, and estimated interference probabilities. The system models realistic conditions of primary user (PU) activity, channel fading, and interference in a dynamically simulated environment, enabling the DQN agent to learn by doing. The proposed model outperforms nonadaptive and rule-based approaches in terms of spectrum use, latency, and overall service quality (QoS). The simulation-based framework provides a solid convergence point for the anticipated hardware integration in next generation wireless networks.

title: Evaluating Machine Learning Methods for DDoS Detection in IDS
authors: Latha R, Caleb S, A.Marimuthu, Sivagami S, Senthilvadivu S
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustainable Computing and Smart Systems (ICSCSS)
abstract: In recent years, the number of cyber threats experienced and their complexity especially DDoS attacks have been on the rise. Usually, traditional Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) based on rule-based or signature-based approach cannot detect new or changing patterns of DDoS attacks. In dealing with this shortcoming, the paper considers some of the machine learning (ML) methods to achieve a better detection rate, flexibility and real-time performance within IDS. We perform a comparative analysis of five supervised learning models: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). The models are evaluated using three benchmark datasets. There is normalization, class balancing, and Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) which is used to reduce dimensionality and feature selection within the data preprocessing pipeline. The evaluation of model performance is based on the standard evaluation measure, real-time measure of detection latency and false positive rate. It is clear that XGBoost is the best performing model with a detection rate of 98.1%, F1-score of 0.95 and the lowest latency of 25 milliseconds executed in a simulated real-time environment. Moreover, feature selection method was used, which reduced train time by up to 42 percent at a cost affordable to the performance, making it effective in resource-limited environments. All in all, the investigation enriches insight into ML-based IDS and proves the feats of XGBoost as a highly effective and practical model to use in detection of DDoS attacks in the design of intelligent real time deterrence of threats.

title: GN2T: A Hybrid Graph Neural and Tree-Structured Architecture for ECG-Based Arrhythmia Recognition
authors: Ashraful Goni, Rubiat Jahan Momtahina
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) arrhythmia classification remains challenging due to heterogeneous signal morphology and overlooked inter-feature dependencies in conventional models. This study introduces GN2T, a hybrid framework that combines correlation-driven graph convolution with tree-structured hierarchical learning. Graph-based adjacency construction captures physiological feature relationships, while CNN-RF hybrid nodes refine temporal patterns through gating mechanisms. Multi-head attention integrates multi-scale representations, enhancing the detection of subtle arrhythmia variations. Tested on four PhysioNet databases—MIT-BIH Arrhythmia, MIT-BIH Supraventricular, INCART 12-lead, and Sudden Cardiac Death Holter—GN2T achieved 98.48%–98.99% accuracy with precision above 98.70%. Comparative results show improvements of up to +2.38% over Random Forest and +9.19% over CNN, while maintaining cross-dataset stability under 1.48% variance. GN2T demonstrates strong potential for robust, clinically reliable arrhythmia detection across diverse populations.

title: Optimized Multiplexer-Based Carry Select Adder Implementation
authors: Parthibaraj Anguraj, Thiruvenkadam Krishnan, Mani P, Vivek Karthick Perumal, Saravanan Subramanian
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: In this work, a MUX-based Carry Select Adder (CSA) is designed to improve throughput and reduce LUT usage. The design combination of multiplexers with logic gates. The multiplexer selects the carry value as either 0 or 1. This method avoids using extra adder blocks. As a result, the hardware area is reduced without affecting the accuracy of the adder. The delay is also reduced, which improves performance. The design is tested for sizes from 8 bits to 128 bits. It is implemented on a Virtex4 FPGA. The results show that the design works well and is efficient.

title: Plant Health Monitoring System of Potato Images: Improved Alexnet Approach
authors: Anchal Kumawat, Biswaranjan Acharya, N. Venkatesh, Pardhu Thottempudi, Rabinarayan Satpathy
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Networks (CINE)
abstract: The agricultural industry encounters substantial obstacles in preserving crop well-being and productivity owing to plant diseases and unfavorable climatic conditions. To address these issues, we employ Deep Learning (DL) techniques to detect unhealthy plant leaves and diagnose diseases. Our approach leverages pre-trained CNN model includes the basic AlexNet model to identify disease. Our study explores the application of DL techniques to the Plant Village dataset, employing an 80:20 training-testing split and implementing data augmentation and preprocessing strategies to optimize model performance. The results demonstrate the superiority of the IAlexNet model over other AlexNet architectures, highlighting the potential of transfer learning in plant disease diagnosis. By automating disease detection, this approach can help reduce manual labor and improve crop yields, contributing to more efficient agricultural practices.

title: Optimal Mass Transport Based Room Geometry Estimation with Acoustic Consistency Constraints
authors: R Yazhini, S Muthu Vasanth, Sudheesh P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Recent Advances in Electrical, Electronics, Ubiquitous Communication, and Computational Intelligence (RAEEUCCI)
abstract: Room geometry estimation from acoustic signals is a fundamental problem in spatial audio processing, indoor localization and room acoustics. In existing literature, most solutions are dependent on peak picking, approximate models of acoustic signal propagation and supervised learning; however, the performance of these models is influenced by model assumptions and data dependencies. Therefore, it is necessary to develop methods that minimize data-dependent assumptions in order to create algorithms that can generalize effectively. In this paper, we propose a novel solution that uses Optimal Mass Transport (OMT) applied to Room Impulse Responses (RIRs). The RIR energy is normalized, which, when subjected to pairwise OMT-based temporal shift modelling, identifies the subtle acoustic energy diffusion differences that exist in room geometries. Expected delay differences are predicted using an image source-based geometric model, accounting for both direct and reflected propagation paths. An intelligent objective function matches OMT-derived delays with geometry-based predictions while adaptively weighting microphone pairs according to their sensitivity to room length, width, and height. Room geometry is estimated using a two-frame network that combines Differential Evolution (DE) with a Basin Hopping (BH) search technique, optimizing the non-convex problem arising from geometric room size identification. The proposed solution is non-linear, training-free, and does not rely on explicit peak picking, making it robust to noise and multipath effects. Experimental simulations on different room configurations demonstrate that the proposed method estimates the room length, width, and height with percentage errors of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.77 \%, 8.33 {\%}$</tex>, and 11.68%, respectively.

title: TriCA-Net: A Tri-Context Adaptive MoE Framework for Brain Metastases Segmentation Using Multimodal MRIs
authors: Ameer Hamza, Robertas Damaševičius, Muhammad Attique Khan, John Abbas, Wardah Ibrar
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Metastases are the most frequent form of intracranial tumor impacting adults and are the greatest challenge to clinical practice due to their multifocal characteristics and heterogeneous appearance in MRIs. Accurate, reproducible segmentation of metastatic lesions is critical for facilitating diagnosis, treatment planning, and post-therapy assessment. However, segmenting brain metastases remains a major challenge owing to the extreme variability in size, shape, boundary uncertainty, and spatial dispersion across patients. To address these challenges, we proposed TriCA-Net, a Tri-Context Adaptive Expert Network based on a heterogeneous MoE framework for multi label segmentation. For the experiments, BratsMet2023 and BratsMet2025 are used, and the proposed method achieved the highest dice scores of 90.66% and 72.14%, respectively. Moreover, the proposed framework has an inference time of 106.5 ms and outperforms several state-of-the-art architectures in both performance and computational efficiency.

title: Enhanced Liver Tumor Segmentation via Optimized Preprocessing and Attention-Based U-Net Model
authors: Carine Shqeir, Mohamad Diab, Racha Soubra
publish: 2026 IEEE 5th International Multidisciplinary Conference on Engineering Technology (IMCET)
abstract: Liver cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide. Accurate liver tumor segmentation from Computed Tomography (CT) images is a critical step for diagnosis, treatment planning, and disease monitoring. However, it is challenging due to heterogeneous tumor appearance, low contrast boundaries, and large variability in lesion size and shape. In this work, an Attention U-Net model integrated with Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) and Convolutional Block Attention Modules (CBAM) is adopted, with a focus on optimizing preprocessing techniques to enhance liver tumor segmentation performance. The preprocessing pipeline includes liver-focused cropping, normalization, gamma correction, and contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization to improve tumor visibility and boundary definition. The model is trained and evaluated on liver-focused CT slices derived from the LiTS dataset. Segmentation performance is assessed using Dice Similarity Coefficient and Intersection over Union metrics. Experimental results achieve a tumor Dice score of 0.9166 and an IoU of 0.8586, demonstrating a substantial improvement over baseline approaches and highlighting the critical role of preprocessing combined with attention-enhanced architectures. The proposed framework provides a robust and accurate approach for automated liver tumor segmentation while maintaining computational efficiency, supporting its potential for automated clinical image analysis.

title: Detection and Classification of Parkinson’s Disease using Machine Learning
authors: Mohsina Mehrin, Md. Rokonuzzaman Mim
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
abstract: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a progressive neurological disorder that affects motor and speech functions. Early detection of PD is very important for proper treatment and disease management. In recent years, machine learning techniques have shown promising performance in disease prediction using biomedical data. In this study, several machine learning algorithms including Support Vector Machine (SVM), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Naïve Bayes, XGBoost, AdaBoost, and Gradient Boost were used to classify Parkinson’s disease using a dataset from the UCI Machine Learning Repository. Initially, the models were trained using the original dataset, but the performance was not satisfactory. Therefore, two feature selection methods, correlation matrix with heatmap and information gain, were applied to improve classification performance. The models were evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. After feature selection, the performance of the models improved significantly. Among all models, XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy while Random Forest showed the highest sensitivity. The results indicate that feature selection plays an important role in improving Parkinson’s disease detection accuracy

title: Feature Aggregation and Multi-Class Detection (FAMCD) Framework for Skin Cancer Classification
authors: Ifjaz Ahmed M, Sahithy Ayyagari, Uma Maheswari M, Premaladha Jayaraman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Innovative Trends in Information Technology (ICITIIT)
abstract: An unwelcome threat to life, melanoma, occurs if it is inconspicuously missed in an early detection stage. This research article presents a smart detection system called FAMCD (Feature Aggregation and Multi-Class Detection-to better detect and classify melanomas). Steps involved in the smart detection system include pre-cleaning such as hair removal from skin images, manual annotation of the lesion (area of suspicion), and then feature extraction of both common features (shape, color, and margin of the lesion) and deep learning features based on classical rule sets such as the ABCD rule (which considers parameters such as shape and color). Additionally, special techniques are incorporated that claim a measurement of uncertainty from the model in its predictions: frequency dropout and Monte Carlo sampling. To better understand the extent of model performance, three new metrics are proposed for evaluation: TUI (Total Uncertainty Index) for quantifying uncertainty level, BPS (Boundary Preservation Score) for verifying how well the lesion edge is preserved, and SCE (Structure Consistency Error) for verifying how well the structure of the lesion is preserved. This experimental evaluation demonstrated that the approach yielded a TUI value of 1.0847, along with a BPS score of 0.4000 (30% high uncertainty) and an SCE of 0.4875 (80% retained data). The system was tested on the popular skin cancer image dataset HAM10000. In terms of classification performance, the method yielded an overall accuracy of 0.8616 and a macro-AUC-ROC score of 0.8151, highlighting strong predictive ability. However, the balanced accuracy (0.3562), macro recall (0.3562), and macro F1-score (0.3519) indicate limitations in handling class imbalance. And the Local Interpretable Model (LIME) generates intuitive visual explanations that enhance physicians understanding of the model's reasoning, thereby fostering greater trust in its decisions.

title: Leveraging VGG16 and ResNet50 for Clinically Aligned Assessment in Breast Cancer Histopathology
authors: Yara Nassar, Mohanad Alayedi
publish: 2026 IEEE 15th International Conference on Communication Systems and Network Technologies (CSNT)
abstract: Recently, deep learning (DL) models have achieved high accuracies on the BreakHis dataset for breast cancer histopathological classification; these results are often inflated by data leakage from non-patient-level splits and idealized conditions. This study conducts a realistic comparative evaluation of VGG16 and ResNet50 transfer learning architectures for binary (e.g., benign against malignant) classification. In this paper, we introduce three clinically motivated protocols: first, strict patientlevel splitting to prevent leakage; second, cross-magnification testing (training on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$40 \times$</tex> only, testing on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100 \times / 200 \times / 400 \times$</tex>); and third, robustness assessment under strong Gaussian noise and blur degradations. Metrics are reported at both the image and patient-level (via probability aggregation). According to simulation results, ResNet50 consistently outperforms VGG16, showing up to 15 % less accuracy degradation in cross-magnification and degradation scenarios due to its residual connections. These experiments reveal a significant performance gap relative to literature benchmarks, advocating for stricter evaluation standards. The proposed framework and splits are released for reproducible research.

title: HS-ANET: Star Spectral Type Enhanced Astrometric Calibration for Hyper Spectral Space Imaging
authors: William Mitchell, Kevin Phan, David Chaparro, Enrique De Alba, J. Zachary Gazak
publish: 2025 15th Workshop on Hyperspectral Imaging and Signal Processing: Evolution in Remote Sensing (WHISPERS)
abstract: Traditional lost-in-space algorithms, such as those implemented in astrometry.net, solve for spacecraft orientation by matching observed star fields to celestial catalogs using geometric asterisms alone. In this work, we propose a novel extension to astrometry.net that incorporates stellar spectral type, which is derived from hyperspectral imagery, into the matching process. By adding this spectral dimension to each star detection, we constrain the search space and improve match specificity, enabling successful astrometric solutions with significantly fewer stars. Our modified pipeline demonstrates improved fit rates and reduced failure cases in cluttered or ambiguous star fields, which is especially critical for autonomous space situational awareness and traffic management. Our results suggest that modest spectral resolution, when incorporated into existing geometric frameworks, can dramatically improve robustness and efficiency in onboard star identification systems.

title: Corruption-Resilient Combinatorial Bandit Learning for Heterogeneous Network Systems
authors: Junkai Wang, Xutong Liu, Jinhang Zuo, Yuedong Xu, John C.S. Lui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: We study online decision-making problems in network applications using the framework of contextual combinatorial multi-armed bandits (C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MAB). Although bandit methods provide a natural solution, two key challenges arise in practice: corruption in feedback and heterogeneity across clients. Corruption may stem from adversarial behaviors or system anomalies, leading to biased reward estimations, while heterogeneity reflects differences in clients’ environments such as computation capabilities or network conditions. To address these challenges, we formulate the C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MAB under corruption (C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>MAB-C) problem and extend it to the setting with heterogeneous environmental parameters. We then propose two novel algorithms, namely CW-C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>UCB, which leverages confidence-weighted estimations to mitigate corruption effects, and CW-C<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>CLUB, which further integrates an online clustering mechanism to adaptively group tasks based on shared structure. We derive tight theoretical regret bounds for both algorithms under various corruption models showing that our upper bounds match the established lower bounds up to logarithmic factors. Empirical results on three real-world applications, content delivery networks, client selection in federated learning, and VR video streaming, demonstrate that our proposed algorithms significantly outperform existing baselines, achieving lower regret and stronger resilience in adversarial environments.

title: TransVort: A Temporally-Coherent Physics-Guided Neural Network for Super-Resolving and Denoising 4D Flow MRI of Cerebrospinal Fluid_supp2-3690957.docx
authors: Vitaly Rayz
publish: N/A
abstract: Objective: To enhance the diagnostic utility of 4D flow MRI in assessing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) dynamics by super-resolving and denoising measured velocities using temporally coherent, physics-guided neural networks (PGNN). Methods: Synthetic 4D flow MRI was generated from 40 computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations across 10 ventricular geometries. These simulations were used to generate paired synthetic 4D flow MRI and high-resolution velocity fields used for supervised training. Here, we compare a previously developed temporally independent network (div-mDCSRN-Flow) using divergence-based regularization with two novel temporal PGNNs (tempo-mDCSRN-Flow using divergence-regularization and TransVort additionally constrained by the vorticity transport equation). Results: In application to synthetic 4D flow MRI of a double gyre flow showed the temporal PGNNs improve vorticity estimation. Similarly, both temporal methods improved estimation of vorticity and time-averaged wall shear stress (TAWSS) of synthetic 4D flow MRI in the 3rd and 4th ventricle. While using temporal PGNNs improves velocity and vorticity quantification across temporal resolutions, TransVort demonstrated additional improvement at fine temporal resolutions. Application of TransVort to in vivo 4D flow MRI of CSF flow captured vortex formation and dissipation in the 4th ventricle over the cardiac cycle. Conclusion: Leveraging the temporal information of 4D flow MRI improves reconstruction of high-resolution velocity fields. This leads to better estimation of gradient-based flow metrics such as vorticity and TAWSS, which are associated with neurodegenerative and neurovascular diseases. Significance: Augmenting the accuracy of 4D flow MRI increases its potential for adoption as a clinical tool for diagnosing and monitoring disorders of neurofluid dynamics.

title: Probing Deeper Zone of Lung using Four Electrode Techniques: An FEM Approach
authors: Mahjabin Mobarak, Habiba Begum, Md. Moniruzzaman, Abdullah Al Mahfazur Rahman, Md. Ruhul Amin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Biomedical Engineering, Computer and Information Technology for Health (BECITHCON)
abstract: Electrical Impedance Techniques are being explored as a low-cost, non-invasive method for detecting lung disorders by measuring bioimpedance differences between healthy and diseased tissue. Air-filled lungs (inhalation) show higher impedance, while fluid-filled or collapsed lungs (exhalation) show lower impedance. Methods like Tetrapolar Impedance Measurement (TPIM) and Focused Impedance Method (FIM) enable localized lung assessment. In this study, Finite Element Method (FEM) simulations using a simplified lung model evaluated the effectiveness of various electrode configurations-implementing electrodes on one side and both sides. Simulation results revealed that the contribution of the lung to the total measured impedance (CLTI) varied significantly depending on the electrode configuration and the respiratory phase. For the both-sided electrode placement, CLTI ranged from 13–24% during inhalation to 23–45% during exhalation, indicating a strong influence of the lung’s condition on the overall impedance. In contrast, the one-sided electrode configuration yielded lower CLTI values, ranging from 7–12% during inhalation to 14–20% during exhalation, suggesting reduced sensitivity to lung-specific impedance changes when current and voltage electrodes are confined to a single side of the thorax. The transfer impedance changes of 28–37% and 10-16% for both sided and one-sided configuration respectively. These findings indicate that the both-sided setup allows deeper current penetration through the thoracic region and greater responsiveness to changes in lung aeration. The results suggest that simplified Electrical Impedance Methods with both sided electrode placement can potentially detect localized lung abnormalities.

title: Automatic Red Teaming LLM-based Agents with Model Context Protocol Tools_supp1-3691201.pdf
authors: Shouling Ji
publish: N/A
abstract: The remarkable capability of large language models (LLMs) has led to the wide application of LLM-based agents in various domains. To standardize interactions between LLM-based agents and external resources, model context protocol (MCP) tools have become the de facto standard and are now widely integrated into these agents. However, the incorporation of MCP tools introduces the risk of tool poisoning attacks, in which malicious MCP tools can steer the behavior of LLM-based agents toward unintended outcomes. Although previous studies have identified such vulnerabilities, their red teaming approaches have largely remained at the proof-of-concept stage, leaving the automatic red teaming of LLM-based agents under the MCP tool poisoning paradigm an open question. To bridge this gap, we propose AutoMalTool, an automated red teaming framework for LLM-based agents by generating malicious MCP tools. Our extensive evaluation shows that AutoMalTool effectively generates malicious MCP tools capable of manipulating the behavior of mainstream LLM-based agents while evading current detection mechanisms, thereby revealing new security risks in these agents.

title: Consumer Data Privacy-Aware Federated Orchestration for Communication, Computing, and Control in 6G Consumer Services
authors: Syed Danial Ali Shah, Ali Kashif Bashir, Daehan Kwak, Amina Salhi, Farman Ali
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Emerging Consumer Electronics (CE) systems, such as connected vehicles, immersive AR/VR environments, cloud gaming platforms, and intelligent home robotics, are increasingly characterized by their dependence on distributed intelligence, seamless connectivity, and autonomous control. These systems often span multiple Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and serElectronicsders, requiring coordinated management of communication, computing, and control resources at the network edge. When AI and RAN workloads share edge infrastructure, this coexistence naturally achieves integration: RAN workloads provide communication and control while AI workloads supply computing intelligence. However, as MNOs increasingly share infrastructure under the Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN) paradigm, efficient resource allocation across heterogeneous workloads and independent Service Level Agreements (SLAs) becomes critical. At the same time, preserving consumer data privacy across administrative boundaries remains a major concern. This paper proposes the Converged AI and O-RAN Architectural (CAORA) framework, a federated and SLA aware orchestration solution that integrates AI and RAN workloads with communication, computing, and control. CAORA employs local spike-aware workload forecasting using attention-based LSTMs and aggregates predictions through federated learning to avoid raw data sharing between operators. A centralized Soft Actor Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning agent then allocates edge resources fairly and efficiently while meeting SLA constraints. Experiments on real 5G traffic traces from Barcelona show that CAORA achieves about 90% task completion, reduces SLA violations to below 10%, and reaches nearly 100% resource utilization under peak load.

title: Multitree Genetic Programming with Behavioral Semantics for Large-Scale Dynamic Air Traffic Flow Management
authors: Tong Guo, Yi Mei, Mengjie Zhang, Hui Ding, Wenbo Du
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Dynamic Air Traffic Flow Management (DATFM) is vital to modern aviation industries, requiring real-time routing and sequencing aircraft within constrained airspace to ensure safety and efficiency, particularly during unforeseen disruptions. Multitree Genetic Programming (MTGP) provides an automatic design framework to simultaneously learn routing and sequencing policies. However, the coupling interdependence between the routing and sequencing decisions in MTGP tends to produce structurally valid but semantically ineffective offspring, thereby limiting MTGP’s search ability within the heuristic space. This paper presents a novel Multitree Genetic Programming with Behavioral Semantics (MrGPBS) approach that automatically evolves effective reactive scheduling heuristics for DATFM. First, we develop a specialized multitree representation incorporating informative terminals that enable concurrent evolution of routing and sequencing decisions, coupled with a problem-tailored heuristic template that decodes these representations into executable solutions in an online manner. Second, we propose a behavioral semantics-guided evolutionary mechanism that addresses the coupling interdependence issue of existing MTGP methods. Our method preserves well-evolved tree structures by promoting recombination between behaviorally compatible individuals, thereby enabling incremental improvement within the heuristic search space. We evaluate MrGPBS on comprehensive benchmark instances derived from real-world air traffic data, encompassing diverse problem scales and dynamic operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

title: D-PolTransGAN: Scattering Guided Hybrid Transformer-GAN for SAR-Optical Image Translation using Dual Polarization SAR
authors: Jagrati Talreja, Tewodros Syum Gebre, Leila Hashemi-Beni
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) provides reliable imaging in all weather and lighting conditions, but its speckled appearance makes visual interpretation difficult and limits its integration with optical-based analyses. To address this challenge, we propose a new SAR-to-optical image translation framework tailored for flood assessment. The proposed model processes SAR VV and VH polarizations through separate feature extraction streams, enabling each stem to learn its unique scattering characteristics. These features are then fused using cross-attention to capture complementary information from both polarizations. The architecture combines convolutional networks for local detail extraction with transformer modules to model global context, all within a generative adversarial learning framework. We train and evaluate the model using the DEEPFLOOD, SEN1FLOODS11 and SEN12MS datasets, which provides well-aligned Sentinel-1 (VV, VH) and Sentinel-2 image pairs. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in terms of PSNR, SSIM, and perceptual quality (LPIPS), while preserving fine spatial details critical for flood analysis. Qualitative results on real flood events further demonstrate that the generated optical-like images recover visual structures and contextual information that are difficult to interpret in raw SAR imagery. <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/JagratiTalreja01/D-PolTransGAN</uri>

title: A Conceptual Study for Prediction of the Floating behavior of Metallic Particle
authors: Seungmin Bang, Chanyeol Ryu, Bang-Wook Lee
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Metallic particle was generated inside the Gas Insulated Switchgear (GIS) due to incorrect assembly process or mechanical operation. During normal operation, the influence of the electric filed causes the metallic particle to float over the conductor, resulting in partial discharge and dielectric breakdown. It is very important to prevent insulation accidents by predicting the floating height of metallic particle. However, the shape and size of metallic particle very diverse, and the floating height varies depending on the type of voltage. Previous studies had analyzed the movement or partial discharge characteristics of metallic particle through experiments. Also, the movement of the metallic particle was performed only through simulation, and were not consistent with the experimental results. Therefore, in this paper, a conceptual study for prediction of the floating behavior of metallic particle. The floating height and lift-off voltage of the metallic particle were investigated according to the shape, size and voltage type. The floating height of metallic particle were simulated by Boundary Element Method (BEM), and the results of the BEM and the experiment were consistent. It is found that lift-off voltage and floating height of the metallic particle could be predicted without conducting experiments.

title: Unlocking Flexibility Potential through DVFS-controlled Data Center Clusters in Unit Commitment
authors: Yiping Yuan, Yufei Teng, Liqian Zhu, Yihang Cao, Zhenyuan Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: Data center clusters (DCCs) have become recognized as significant energy consumers in electrical power system oper-ations. Workload reallocation processes for multiple DCCs, im-plemented by dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) control schemes, can provide flexibility potential capabilities and contribute to maintaining the security of power systems opera-tions through Unit Commitment (UC). To achieve this, The flexi-bility potential of workload reallocation hosted by multiple DCCs is analyzed. Building on this analysis, an interval-based work-load reallocation strategy is extended to depict flexibility poten-tial from workload response while avoiding service-time latency. Then, an innovation set of physical security constraints repre-senting DVFS-controlled DCCs is developed, and related special-ordered sets-of-type-1 (SOS1) linearized constraints are derived through the McCormick Envelopes method. Furthermore, these intractable constraints are incorporated into UC, and an en-hanced UC model, which considers the flexibility potential offered by DVFS-controlled DCCs, is developed. Several experiments are conducted on the IEEE 6 buses test systems to assess the validity and scalability of the proposed models.

title: Towards Robust Alignment for Video Dehazing with Temporal Lookup Table
authors: Haoyou Deng, Zhiqiang Li, Feng Zhang, Bin Xu, Qingbo Lu, Changxin Gao, Nong Sang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Video dehazing aims to restore clean scenarios from a sequence of hazy frames, where frame alignment is a critical stage for leveraging temporal information. However, haze degrades contrast and obscures details, making alignment challenging. Existing methods ignore the impairment of haze on alignment and thus struggle to align frames accurately. To address this challenge, we propose an alignment network with the temporal lookup table (temporal-LUT), which effectively enhances the haze-degraded frames and provides vivid cues for precise alignment. Specifically, to tackle the color degradation of haze, we employ a learnable lookup table (LUT) to enhance hazy color. The color mapping nature of LUT favorably preserves the naturalness of enhanced outcomes. Besides, we introduce a temporal weight prediction strategy to strengthen inter-frame interaction, which ensures temporal consistency across enhanced results and thereby benefits alignment. Extensive experimental results on two widely used benchmarks and real-world scenes demonstrate the superiority of our method.
